:alt: The dolphin-pony - proof that cute + cute = double cute.

%

> The dolphin-pony - proof that cute + cute = double cute.

Django-MySQL extends Django's built-in MySQL and MariaDB support their specific
features not available on other databases.

## What kind of features?

Includes:

- `QuerySet` extensions:

  - 'Smart' iteration - chunked pagination across a large queryset
  - `approx_count` for quick estimates of `count()`
  - Query hints
  - Quick `pt-visual-explain` of the underlying query

- Model fields:

  - MariaDB Dynamic Columns for storing dictionaries
  - Comma-separated fields for storing lists and sets
  - 'Missing' fields: differently sized `BinaryField`/`TextField` classes,
    `BooleanField`s represented by BIT(1)

- ORM expressions for over 20 MySQL-specific functions

- A new cache backend that makes use of MySQL's upsert statement and does
  compression

- Status variable inspection and utility methods

- Named locks for easy locking of e.g. external resources

- Table lock manager for hard to pull off data migrations

To see them all, check out the exposition at
<https://django-mysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/exposition.html> .

## Requirements and Installation

Please see
<https://django-mysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html> .

## Documentation

Every detail documented on
[Read The Docs](https://django-mysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

classes.name: django_mysql.test.utils.override_mysql_variables
documentation:
Based on Django's override_settings, but for connection settings. Give a
connection alias in using and variable=value pairs to save on that
connection. Keeps the old values MySQL-side using session variables of the
form @overridden_X.

Acts as either a decorator, or a context manager. If it's a decorator it
takes a function and returns a wrapped function. If it's a contextmanager
it's used with the `with` statement. In either event entering/exiting
are called before and after, respectively, the function/block is executed.

methods.name: django_mysql.locks.TableLock._process_names
documentation:
Convert a list of models/table names into a list of table names. Deals
with cases of model inheritance, etc.

Adds an arbitrary user-defined comment that will appear after
SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE which can be used to identify where the query was
generated, etc.
Query rewrite/hint API
These specially constructed comments will be rewritten by rewrite_query
into actual hints

modules.name: django_mysql.rewrite_query
documentation:
Implements a function for hoisting specially constructed comments in SQL
queries and using them to rewrite that query. This is done to support query
hints whilst obviating patching Django's ORM in complex ways.

functions.name: django_mysql.rewrite_query.modify_sql
documentation:
Parse the start of the SQL, injecting each string in add_comments in
individual SQL comments after the first keyword, and adding the named
SELECT hints from add_hints, taking the latest in the list in cases of
multiple mutually exclusive hints being given

functions.name: django_mysql.utils.settings_to_cmd_args
documentation:
Copied from django 1.8 MySQL backend DatabaseClient - where the runshell
commandline creation has been extracted and made callable like so.

functions.name: django_mysql.utils.index_name
documentation:
Returns the name of the index existing on field_names, or raises KeyError
if no such index exists.

classes.name: django_mysql.utils.WeightedAverageRate

methods.name: django_mysql.utils.WeightedAverageRate.__init__
documentation:
target_t - Target time for t in update()
weight - Weight of previous n/t values

methods.name: django_mysql.utils.WeightedAverageRate.update
documentation:
Update weighted average rate.  Param n is generic; it's how many of
whatever the caller is doing (rows, checksums, etc.).  Param s is how
long this n took, in seconds (hi-res or not).

Parameters:
    n - Number of operations (rows, etc.)
    t - Amount of time in seconds that n took

Returns:
    n adjusted to meet target_t based on weighted decaying avg rate

Adapted from percona-toolkit - provides a weighted average counter to keep
at a certain rate of activity (row iterations etc.).

classes.name: django_mysql.utils.StopWatch
documentation: Context manager for timing a block

functions.name: django_mysql.apps.install_rewrite_hook
documentation:
Rather than use the documented API of the `execute_wrapper()` context
manager, directly insert the hook. This is done because:
1. Deleting the context manager closes it, so it's not possible to enter it
   here and not exit it, unless we store it forever in some variable.
2. We want to be idempotent and only install the hook once.

classes.name: django_mysql.status.BaseStatus
documentation: Base class for the status classes

functions.name: django_mysql.cache.default_reverse_key_func
django_mysql.cache.get_reverse_key_func

type.name: str | Callable[[str], tuple[str, str, int]] | None

documentation:
Function to decide which reverse key function to use

Defaults to `None`, as any other value might not apply to the given
KEY_FUNCTION. Also the user may not use any of the operations that require
reversing the key_func.

classes.name: django_mysql.cache.Options
documentation:
A class that will quack like a Django model _meta class.

This allows cache operations to be controlled by the router
Slightly modified copies of Options/BaseDatabaseCache from django's
cache.backends.db - these allow us to act like a separate app for database
routers (django_mysql), and not appear on django's `createcachetable`
command
